content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 17, 2002

Mark Anthony Dierolf
PO Box 6879
Salinas, CA 93912-6879

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-02-175

Dear Mr. Dierolf:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. May a county general purpose ballot measure committee be formed to support and oppose measures regarding local taxation?
2. May you, an elected officeholder, chair a general purpose ballot measure committee?
3. If not, may you work with the committee as its spokesperson and assist in fundraising?
4. Are there any special reporting requirements for this type of committee?

CONCLUSIONS

1. The Act does not prohibit the formation of county general purpose ballot measure committees.
2. As long as the proposed committee does not support or oppose candidates, there are no restrictions on your involvement with the committee; you may act as the committee's chair. Once you leave public office and terminate your campaign committee, and assuming there are no other candidates controlling

_______________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-02-175
Page No. 2

the committee, the committee may support or oppose candidates as well as
ballot measures.
3. As stated in conclusion 2 above, you may act as the spokesperson of a general
purpose ballot measure committee and assist in fundraising for the committee.
4. General purpose ballot measure committees may have special reporting
obligations. See the enclosed copy of the Information Manual and addendum
for the reporting obligations of these types of committees.

FACTS

You and other community members want to form a recipient committee that
would exist to support and oppose ballot measures pertaining to taxation that appear on
the ballot in various jurisdictions in Monterey County. You intend to chair the
committee. You are an elected member of a community college board in Monterey
County and currently control a city ballot measure committee supporting a specific
measure appearing on the November 2002 ballot.

You are not currently running for office and do not intend to run for public office
in the future. By the time the proposed general purpose ballot measure committee is
formed, all your controlled committees will be terminated.

ANALYSIS

Section 82013(a) defines a committee as "any person or combination of persons
who directly or indirectly … [r]eceives contributions totaling one thousand dollars
($1,000) or more in a calendar year." Since your committee will not be primarily formed
to support a single measure or "two or more measures being voted upon in the same city,
county, multicounty, or state election," pursuant to section 82047.5, it will qualify as a
general purpose committee. (Section 82027.5) Section 82027.5(c) defines a county
general purpose committee as:

"… a committee to support or oppose candidates or measures
voted on in only one county, or in more than one jurisdiction
within one county."

The Commission has determined that a candidate² may control a ballot measure
committee (Karpel Advice Letter, No. A-93-356 and Olson Advice Letter, No. A-89-
363), as long as the committee does not make contributions to support or oppose
candidates, including the candidate controlling the ballot measure committee. (Weems
Advice Letter, No. A-91-448.)³ As long as you remain a candidate, as defined by section

² The term "candidate" includes those who hold an elected office. (Sections 82007, 84214;
Regulation 18404(d).)

³ Under the Act, a candidate "controls" a committee whenever the candidate or his or her agent has
a "significant influence on the actions or decisions of the committee." (Section 82016.) For example, we
have advised that when a candidate is a voting member of a committee's leadership, such as the chair of the

File No. A-02-175
Page No. 3

82007, the proposed ballot measure committee will be considered controlled by you.
Section 82007 states, in pertinent part, that "[a]n individual who becomes a candidate
shall retain his or her status as a candidate until such time as that status is terminated
pursuant to Section 84214." Thus, when you terminate your status as a candidate by
leaving office and terminating your campaign committee, then the proposed ballot
measure committee would no longer be considered a committee controlled by a
candidate, namely, yourself. (Bagatelos Advice Letter, No. A-93-104.) At that time, the
proposed committee would be required to file an amended Statement of Organization
(Form 410) to reflect that change.

The required contents of campaign statements are found in section 84211. In
addition, other reports may be required depending upon the committee's activities. The
enclosed Information Manual D specifies the reporting obligations of county general
purpose ballot measure committees.

If you have any additional questions, please call me at (866) 275-3772.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Kevin S. Moen, PhD
Political Reform Consultant II
Technical Assistance Division

Enclosures

committee, the candidate is presumed to be exerting significant influence on the committee. (Ferguson
Advice Letter, No. A-86-044.)
